§6Xenon-135§f

§aThe reactor poison — a neutron-absorbing fission product that can stall your RBMK!§f

§bXenon-135§f (Xe-135) is an extremely radioactive and dangerous isotope responsible for poisoning nuclear reactors (specifically the RBMK) when on low power. It is so radioactive (§e3,750 RAD/s§f) that it is naturally §epyrophoric§f.

§6Production§f: In an RBMK, when fuel receives low neutron flux, Xe-135 builds up faster than it is burned away. Only §e1%§f chance to extract a tiny pile when recycling poisoned fuel in a SILEX.

§6Xenon Poisoning Mechanics§f:
§b• Xenon is gained§f at rate defined by the fuel's xenon gen function (usually ×0.5)
§b• Xenon is burned§f at rate defined by xenon burn function (usually ×²/25)
§b• At equilibrium§f: a fuel emitting >25 flux stays xenon-free
§b• At 100% poisoning§f: no flux reaches the rod at all
§b• Immune fuels§f: neutron sources and some late-game fuels ignore xenon

§6Effects§f:
• Reduces fuel reactivity — can render rods nearly inert
• Reduces yields from spent RBMK fuel processing
• Built-up xenon can cause dangerous flux spikes when burned off too aggressively

§6Management§f: If Xe-135 accumulates, expose the fuel to a neutron source to burn it off before disassembly. This slightly reduces other recycling products.

§6Currently§f: Xe-135 has no uses beyond combining 9 tiny piles into 1 powder. It's pure waste to manage.
